We recommended that
the Municipal Mayor
direct the:
a. Inventory Committee
to prepare a Physical
Inventory Plan and
accordingly conduct a
comprehensive Plan and the
physical inventory
of all PPE of the
Municipality and
prepare the
corresponding Report
on the Physical Count
of Property, Plant and
Equipment (RPCPPE)
following the
guidelines set forth in
COA Circular No.
2020-006;
b. Municipal General
Services Officer
(MGSO) to update his

c. MGSO and Municipal
Accountant to closely

reconcile their

observations.managementAction:
The Inventory Committee had not prepared the
corresponding Report
of the Physical Count
of Property, Plant and
Equipment (RPCPPE)
was still incomplete.

The MGSO had not updated his property records.

The MGSO and Municipal Accountant had not reconciled their respective property records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE accounts to be considered as the correct balance of the Municipality’s PPE.
observations.reason:
It was accepted that the Acting General Services Officer and his team members were not able to conduct
physical inventory of PPE for the reason that the team members have
other job assignments which hamper their conduct of the inventory.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor
direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to maintain Supplies Ledger Cards for each
commodity/stock pursuant to Section 114 of said MNGAS; and
b. GSO to maintain inventory stock cards, conduct complete physical count of all
inventory items at the end of every semester and submit a Report of the
Physical Count of Inventory to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and
January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant were not able to implement the previous year’s and current
year’s recommendation due to lack of personnel in the Office of the Municipal Accountant.
The Acting GSO is also a one-man team with various tasks being performed for the Office
of the Mayor and as BAC Secretary.
observations.reason:
The Municipal Accountant were not able to implement the previous year’s and current
year’s recommendation due to lack of personnel in the Office of the Municipal Accountant.
The Acting GSO is also a one-man team with various tasks being performed for the Office
of the Mayor and as BAC Secretary.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, to review
the list of stale checks to determine that the checks had not been replaced,
particularly those significant in amount like payable to Meralco amounting to
P142,031.42 and for claims which are determined to be still valid to date, to
prepare accounting entries to take up the cancellation of stale checks and
restoration of the accounts payable, subject to compliance with the guidelines
prescribed under Section 59 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
b. Municipal Treasurer to henceforth prepare and send written notice to the
payees of the existence of their checks at least one month before a check
becomes stale.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Inventory Committee
composed of adequate number of members to be able to complete the physical
inventory in three months or less, and who shall be temporarily relieved of all their
regular duties to devote their full time in the conduct of the physical inventory
taking until the same is completed, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-006.
After which, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan and accordingly
conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all PPE of the Municipality and
prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and
Equipment (RPCPPE) following the guidelines set forth in said COA Circular;
b. Acting General Services Officer to update his property records based on the
RPCPPE; and
c. Acting General Services Officer and Municipal Accountant to closely work
together to reconcile their respective property records to come up with the
reconciled balances of PPE accounts to be considered as the correct balance
of the Municipality’s PPE.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor
direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to maintain Supplies Ledger Cards for each
commodity/stock pursuant to Section 114 of said MNGAS; and
b. GSO to maintain inventory stock cards, conduct complete physical count of all
inventory items at the end of every semester and submit a Report of the
Physical Count of Inventory to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and
January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
draw journal entries to record the cost of infrastructure projects in the books of the
General Fund by debiting Road Network and crediting Government Equity in
the amount P7,976,292.53.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
the Bids and Awards Committee to submit to the Audit Team for further evaluation
the lacking documents pertaining to the subject expenditures with total amount of
P1,213,809.00 to avoid suspension and/or disallowance in audit and thereafter
ensure that all documentary requirements are attached to the disbursement
vouchers prior to payment to establish the validity and propriety of the claim.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator and other concerned officials to prepare a catch-up plan
on projects that had been stalled or delayed due to pandemic-linked lockdowns in
the country.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to invite the Auditors
and other observers in writing to also observe the bid evaluation and post-
qualification proceedings of the BAC pursuant to Section 13.1 of the 2016 R-IRR of
RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
submit immediately to the COA Audit Team the 104 DVs, including the supporting
documents, totaling P11,381,158.29 to facilitate the audit thereof, otherwise the
disbursements will be suspended in audit.

We also reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal
Accountant be directed to submit to the Audit Team within 10 days from receipt all
paid vouchers and their supporting documents pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-
006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee
and the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately to the Audit Team the lacking
documents for infrastructure projects procured by the Municipality in the total
amount of P10,501,692.26 CY 2021 to avoid suspension and disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to
release the barangay’s share from RPT within five days after the end of each
quarter as mandated under Section 271 (d) of LGC and Section 15 of MNGAS for
LGUs, Volume I.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to verify the discrepancy in the Due To and Due From accounts of the Municipality with that of its reciprocal accounts in the books of the Province and likewise review the records of the Barangay pertaining to these accounts and to make the necessary adjustments to correct the balances; b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to request the Provincial Accountant to furnish her with copies of the Journal Entry Vouchers showing the amount collected by the Province in favor of the Municipality to serve as basis in recording the Due from LGU in the books of the Municipality as provided under Section 20 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant will coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to resolve the discrepancy in the reciprocal accounts. Likewise, she will request the Provincial Accountant to furnish her with copies of JEV as basis in recording Due from LGU. Corollary, she will furnish the Provincial Accountant with copies of JEVs on the amounts collected by the Municipality in favor of the Province.
observations.reason: Reiterated in observation No. 3, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:

a. Inventory Committee to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan and accordingly conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all PPE of the Municipality and prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) following the guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006.

b. Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to update his property records based on the RPCPPE.

c. MGSO and Municipal Accountant to closely work together to reconcile their respective property records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE accounts to be considered as the correct balance of the Municipality’s PPE.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Accountant to maintain Supplies Ledger Cards for each commodity/stock pursuant to Section 114 of said MNGAS; and

b. GSO to maintain inventory stock cards, conduct complete physical count of all inventory items at the end of every semester and submit a Report of the Physical Count of Inventory to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to resolve the discrepancy in the Due to LGUs and Due from LGUs account of the Municipality with the corresponding reciprocal account in the books of the Province and thereafter make the necessary adjustments;

b. request the Provincial Accountant to furnish her with copies of the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVS) showing the amounts collected by the Province in favor of the Municipality to serve as basis in recording the Due from LGU in the books of the Municipality; and

c. furnish the Provincial Accountant with copies of JEVs showing the amounts collected by the Municipality in favor of the Province to serve as basis in recording the Due from LGU in the books of the Province.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the GL and SL balances of Due to BIR and Due to NGA accounts to ascertain their accuracy and reliability.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to disclose the required information on various accounts in the NTFS to assist users in better understanding the financial statements and to provide them with additional information value.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to provide sufficient disclosure of the LDDRMF in the Notes to Financial Statements in the ensuing year pursuant to Item 5.1.16 of the COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor limit the grant of cash advances to Municipal officials and employees to circumstances allowed under COA Circular No. 97-002 or when payment thru checks will not be practicable to ensure that government funds are safeguarded from possible losses or misappropriations and applicable taxes due from government money payments to suppliers/creditors are withheld pursuant to Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 23-2014.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. BAC Secretariat to submit to the Audit Team justifications on its inability to comply with the posting requirement under GPPB Circular No. 01-2020, for further evaluation.

b. BAC Secretariat and the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the Mayor’s or Business Permit, Income/Business Tax Returns, and OSS of the suppliers pertaining to COVID-19 expenditures totaling P7,505,965.00, to avoid issuance of audit suspension.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Accountant and the BAC Secretariat to submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents to support the procurement of rescue and motor vehicles in the total amount of P5,896,000.00 within 10 days from receipt of this Report to avoid suspension of the transactions;

b. Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to enforce the collection of liquidated damages of P469,452.00 from the concerned supplier and to take the necessary legal remedies should the supplier refuse to voluntary settle the amount; and

c. Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with Item No. 3 of Annex D of the RIRR by computing and deducting the amount of liquidated damages from claims of suppliers who incur delays in the delivery of goods to the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the Office Orders or any equivalent document from the Municipal Mayor showing authority of regular personnel to report for work during ECQ and their approved DTRs during said quarantine period to enable the Auditors to determine the correctness of the amount paid for COVID-19 Hazard Pay.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately to the Audit Team the 120 unsubmitted DVs including the supporting documents totaling P24,691,107.91 for further evaluation, otherwise the disbursements will be suspended in audit.

Henceforth, it is recommended that the Municipal Accountant be directed to submit to the Audit Team within 10 days from receipt all paid vouchers and their supporting documents as required by COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting further cash advances to personnel with outstanding cash advances and to institute measures to ensure that cash advances are liquidated on time.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. strictly comply with BIR RMC No. 23-2012 by ensuring that the amount of all taxes withheld are remitted in full on or before the 15th day of the following month to prevent the incurrence of surcharges and penalties.

b. cause the immediate remittance of the amount of P3,683,759.17 to the BIR.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to revert back to the General Fund the unexpended balance of Calendar Years 2012 to 2015 LDRRMF in the total amount of P2,975,321.66 to be made available for other social services, in conformity with Section 21 and 5.1.13 of RA No. 10121 and COA Circular No. 2012-002, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee and/or other concerned Municipal officials to:

a. immediately undertake the procurement for the construction of isolation facilities with total budget of P6,981,052.92.

b. plan, discuss and identify the COVID response program with lump-sum budget of P1,492,609.00 which need urgent implementation and prepare and submit the necessary reports for consideration of the Local Development Council in the supplemental investment program to be approved by the Sanggunian Bayan.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point System and other concerned Municipal Officials to submit written explanation to the Auditor for allowing the utilization of GAD fund for non-GAD related activities.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Point System and other concerned Municipal Officials to ensure that only programs, projects and activities with unequivocal relevance to GAD are included in the GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and GAD Accomplishment Report.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point System to cause the posting of GAD fund utilization in conspicuous places and website of the Municipality in compliance with the full disclosure policy of the government.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) Inventory Committee to facilitate the conduct of complete physical count of all PPE and prepare and submit the RPCPPE to ascertain the accuracy, required Physical Inventory Plan.
observations.managementAction: The Management will do its best to implement the recommendations of the Auditor by creating a new inventory team that will prepare an inventory plan to be implemented within the year 2021. The MGSO and the Municipal Accountant will reconcile their respective property records.
observations.reason: Concerned officials did not provide explanation on implementation of the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) Direct the Municipal Assessor to coordinate with the concerned Register of Deeds and/or Bureau of Lands to ensure that the five unbooked real properties which are only covered by Tax Declarations are not covered by existing Certificates of Title and if they are not, facilitate the application for Original Certificates of Title; b) Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain RPLC for land account; and c) Direct the Municipal Assessor and the Municipal Accountant to exert effort in securing all pertinent documents to facilitate the registration and transfer of the title of the land properties from the previous owners to the Municipality of Ternate.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant had prepared and maintained RPLC for Land account. The Municipal Assessor searched for pertinent documents in the records room. On-going retrieval of documents date back to year 2006.
observations.reason: Concerned officials did not provide explanation on partial implementation of the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. The Municipality had unliquidated CAs of P6,138,849.73 as of year-end, of which P5,960,880.98 or 97 per cent pertains to previous years’ CAs, therefore overstating the advances accounts and understating the related expenses accounts. Henceforth, it is recommended that the Municipal Accountant refrain from CAs to all elected official except for their official travels. b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to establish the correct breakdown or schedule of the Advances to Officers and Employees account as of December 31, 2019 and to ensure that all accountable officers with unliquidated cash advances at year-end are included therein; c. Direct the concerned officials and employees to ensure that all cash advances granted to them are liquidated within the prescribed period. d. Direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or employee without liquidating first his/her prior cash advance and to designate another employee as Special Disbursing officer if it is necessary to make additional CAs for special activities/projects. e. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to increase the amount of bond of the subject AO to cover the large amount of her CAs pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to protect the interest of the Municipal Government, or limit the amount of her CAs up to the maximum cash accountability of her fidelity bond. f. Stop the practice of advancing cash other than for Intelligence Fund and official travels.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant had adjusted the SLs with negative balance. The Municipal Accountant had regularly sent demand letters to AOs who failed to liquidate their CAs. The amount of bond of Accountable was already increased pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009. The grant of CAs other than for Intelligence Fund and officials travels were discontinued.
observations.reason: There were CAs that remained unliquidated at year-end. Reiterated in observation No. 11, Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to adjust the RPT and SET receivables by debiting receivables and crediting Deferred Income (RPT and SET) by the same amount to reflect the correct balances of the accounts; b. Adopt additional measures like deputizing the barangay treasurers, conducting collection caravans and other strategies to improve the collection efficiency of the Municipality of Ternate and to abate the continued yearly increase/accumulation of uncollected RPT and SET receivables; c. Initiate the process and take the leadership role on the execution of New Memoranda of Agreement (MOA) with Puerto Azul Management and Caylabne (Exchange Properties) for the settlement of their RPT and SET dues covering CYs 2013-2019.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant adjusted the RPT and SET receivables by debiting receivables and crediting Deferred Income (RPT and SET) by the same amount. The COVID-19 pandemic limited the operation of the concerned Municipal Officials.
observations.reason: The COVID-19 pandemic limited the operation of the concerned Municipal Officials.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: Demand letters to concerned accountable officers were already issued for the immediate settlement of receivables arising from collections in previous years and to file appropriate charges against those who would refuse to settle their obligations to the Municipality. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant facilitate the filing of request for authority from the Commission on Audit for the write-off of her account amounting to P41,954.00, subject to compliance with all the conditions provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters to officers and employees were already issued for the immediate and full settlement of receivables but charges were not filed against those who refused to settle. The Municipal Accountant located the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth files and/or requested from the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth Offices for records or certification which resulted in full settlement of liabilities to said agencies.
observations.reason: There were demand letters already issued for the immediate settlement of receivables but charges were not filed against those who refused to settle their obligations to the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure propriety in the management of the PCF for CY 2019 by: a. Directing the PCF Custodian to liquidate all the PCF under his custody within 20 days after the end of the year; b. Limiting the amount of PCF to what is only sufficient to cover the non-recurring, emergency and petty operating expenses for one month; c. Requiring the Petty Cash Fund (PCF) Custodian and the Municipal Accountant to observe the proper replenishment and liquidation of the PCF and use the forms prescribed such as PCVs and Report of Disbursements supported with summary/list of PCVs; d. Directing the Municipal Accountant to properly record the transactions pertaining to the PCF using the prescribed accounting entries; e. Instructing the PCF Custodian to refrain from making payments of more than P15,000.00 per transaction out of the PCF, otherwise such disbursements will be disallowed in audit; f. Directing the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the 154 vouchers and unsubmitted vouchers totaling P399,167.08 to the Office of the Auditor to avoid suspension of related transactions.
observations.managementAction: The PCF Custodian had liquidated the CA within the reglementary period. The PCF was set up at P50,000.00 a month. Replenishment vouchers were supported with PCVs and Summary list of PCVs. The Municipal Accountant had processed properly the transactions pertaining to the PCF using the prescribed accounting entries. The PCF custodian had already discontinued payments out of the PCF that are more than P15,000.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to grant CAs equivalent only to the net amount of payroll for the specific pay period such as every 15th and end of the month; b. Direct the Disbursing Officer to ensure that receipts of salaries, wages and other personnel benefits by concerned employees are properly acknowledged by their signatures; c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to make sure that liquidations for advances for payrolls are properly supported with required documentations such as DTRs and duly acknowledged payrolls/vouchers.
observations.managementAction: The Disbursing Officer ensures that receipts of salaries, wages and other personnel benefits by concerned employees are acknowledged by their signatures in the payrolls and to observe the prescribed date of liquidation for advances for payroll, which is five days after the pay period; The Municipal Accountant submitted the liquidation reports supported with the required documentations such as DTRs and duly acknowledged payrolls/vouchers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Direct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to strictly comply with Revenue Regulation No. 2-98, RA No. 8291, IRR of RA Nos. 7875 and 9679 by ensuring that the amount of all contributions and taxes withheld are remitted in full on prescribed due dates; b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to continue to make representation with both BIR officials to reconcile both their records in order to establish the exact withholding taxes due for remittance; c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to exert efforts to locate old GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth files and/or request from the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth Offices for records or certification of arrears or no-arrears, to facilitate remittance or adjustments in the books of the previous years’ outstanding unremitted balances.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant had adjusted the subsidiary ledgers for Due to BIR, Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG and Due to PhilHealth. The Municipal Accountant located the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth files and requested records which resulted in full settlement of liabilities to said agencies.
observations.reason: Quarantine restriction limited the operations of Municipal Officials.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Direct the concerned officials and personnel to exercise prudence in the utilization of funds intended for meals and snacks during official reception and programs, meetings, and other entertainment and observe the limitations or prohibitions prescribed by Section 343 of RA No. 7160; b. Direct the Municipal Officials and personnel to charge the costs of meals and snacks for respective visitors/guests of their concerned offices to their monthly representation allowances, which were granted to them for that very purpose; c. Direct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to charge the amount of discretionary/extraordinary expenses to the appropriate account (Extraordinary and Miscellaneous Expenses account); and d. Limit the amount spent for extraordinary discretionary/extraordinary expenses to the amount equivalent to two per cent of the actual receipts of Real Property Taxes of the next preceding calendar year, to avoid suspension or possible disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction: The costs of meals and snacks were already charged to their monthly representation allowances, which were granted to the concerned Municipal Officials and personnel. Discretionary and extraordinary disbursements were no longer incurred.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor initiate the formulation of policies or guidelines on the availment and the entitlement of cellular phone allowances of Municipal officials and employees, subject to compliance with other requirements.
observations.managementAction: Office of the Mayor has been busy with COVID-19 response activities. Policies on the availment and entitlement of cellular phone allowances of Municipal officials and employees were already indorsed to the Sanggunian Bayan.
observations.reason: Office of the Mayor has been busy with COVID-19 response activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and concerned officials and personnel adhere strictly to Section 93 of PD No. 1445 which requires government agencies to make direct payments to suppliers and ensure that the provisions of RA No. 9184 on the appropriate method of procurement and BIR Revenue Regulation 2-98 on withholding of taxes are not circumvented. Further, ensure that reimbursements are supported with proper documentation.
observations.managementAction: Reimbursement of expenses were discontinued.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Direct the concerned officials to study the feasibility of instituting a policy or procedure wherein officials and employees who need to travel on official business may use or borrow any available municipality-owned motor vehicle on a scheduled basis to lessen the need to hire private motor vehicles; and c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to make sure that all claims and/or reimbursements of motor vehicle rentals are supported with proper and complete documentations such as official receipts, contracts, identification cards of drivers or service providers, travel orders, certificates of appearance, certificates of travel completed and other documents that will prove the necessity and propriety of the transactions.
observations.managementAction: Reimbursements, even though few, were already discontinued. Municipality-owned vehicles were strictly used for official travels instead of renting private vehicles. Reimbursements, though few, were already processed with proper and complete documentations.

Advising the MPDC to monitor and evaluate the implementation of the projects and submit periodic reports to the Municipal Mayor incorporating all issues and concerns affecting the execution of projects, copy furnished the COA; and e. Advising the MPDC to monitor and evaluate the implementation of the projects and submit periodic reports to the Municipal Mayor incorporating all issues and concerns affecting the execution of projects, copy furnished the COA.
observations.managementAction: Most of unimplemented projects were re-aligned to COVID-19 related programs. The Bids and Awards Committee explained that bidding for the procurement of isolation facilities had been undertaken during the year 2021, while the Municipal Planning and Development Officer will identify the COVID response program needed to be immediately implemented.
observations.reason: Most of the unimplemented projects were re-aligned to COVID-19 related programs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Direct the Local School Board (LSB) to limit the utilization of the SEF only to PPAs enumerated under DEPED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2017; b. Direct the Municipal Budget Officer to restore the amounts inappropriately charged from the General Fund to SEF to correct the noted deficiency, otherwise, this will be disallowed in audit; c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to submit all disbursement vouchers and liquidation reports to the Office of the Auditor amounting to P4,879,603.72, to avoid suspension of said transactions; and d. Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all disbursements bear the approval of the Municipal Mayor or his authorized representative and other proper authorities, otherwise the transactions will be suspended in audit.
observations.managementAction: The LSB limited the utilization of SEF to PPAs enumerated under DEPED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2017. Fund transfer was made from General Fund to SEF to correct the noted deficiency. The Municipal Accountant submitted all disbursement vouchers and liquidation reports to the Office of the Auditor.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Inventory Team to facilitate the conduct of complete physical count of all
PPE and prepare and submit the Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and
Equipment (RPCPPE) to ascertain the accuracy, validity, existence and conditions
of the recorded PPEs;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to maintain complete and updated Property,
Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards for all PPE of the Municipality;
c. Require the General Services Officer to maintain individual Property Cards for
all PPE of the Municipality; and
d. Require the Accounting Department and General Services Office to undertake the
reconciliation of its records and make the adjustments where needed to arrive at
the correct balance of the PPE account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. the Municipal Assessor to coordinate with the concerned Register of Deeds
and/or Bureau of Lands to ensure that the five unbooked real properties which are
only covered by Tax Declarations are not covered by existing Certificates of Title
and if they are not, facilitate the application for Original Certificates of Titles with
the latter. We recommended that the Municipal Assessor report on the results of
her inquiry with said offices to the Audit Team and to the Municipal Accountant to
enable them to further act on the matter.
b. the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain Real Property Ledger Cards
(RPLC) for land account; and
c. the Municipal Assessor and the Municipal Accountant to exert effort in securing
all pertinent documents to facilitate the registration and transfer of the title of the
land properties from the previous owners to the Municipality of Ternate.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned
officers/employees to immediately liquidate their outstanding cash advances
and/or make the necessary adjustments thereon in the Municipality’s books of
accounts and henceforth ensure that all cash advances are liquidated at year-
end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
exert efforts to establish the correct breakdown or schedule of the Advances
to Officers and Employees accounts as of December 31, 2019 and to ensure
that all accountable officers with unliquidated advances at year-end are
included therein.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain Subsidiary
Ledgers for Advances accounts on a per individual accountable/disbursing
officer basis to facilitate effective recording and monitoring of cash advances.
Likewise, it was recommended that she review the SLs with negative balance
and make the necessary adjustments to correct the balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct concerned officials and
employees to ensure that all cash advances granted to them are liquidated
within the prescribed period. Further, we recommended that he require the
Municipal Accountant to regularly send demand letters to officers and
employees who would fail to liquidate their advances on due dates with a
warning that their salaries would be withheld should they still fail to liquidate
after demand.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or employee
without liquidating first his/her prior cash advance and to designate another
employee as Special Disbursing Officer if it is necessary to make additional
cash advances for special activities/projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to
increase the amount of bond of the subject Accountable Officer to cover the
large amount of her cash advances pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009
to protect the interest of the Municipal Government, or limit the amount of her
cash advances up to the maximum cash accountability of her fidelity bond.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop the practice of advancing
cash except in connection with Intelligence Fund and official travels.
Henceforth, it is recommended that the Municipal Accountant refrain from
granting cash advances to all elected officials except for their official travels.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to adjust the RPT and SET receivables by
debiting P4,844,934.61 and crediting Deferred Income (RPT and SET) by the
same amount to reflect the correct balances of the accounts;
b. Adopt additional measures like deputizing the barangay treasurers,
conducting collection caravans and other strategies to improve the
collection efficiency of the Municipality of Ternate and to abate the
continued yearly increase/accumulation of uncollected RPT and SET
receivables;
c. Initiate the process and take the leadership role on the execution of New
Memoranda of Agreement (MOA) with Puerto Azul Management and
Caylabne Management (Exchange Properties) for the settlement of their RPT
and SET dues covering CYs 2013-2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to verify the discrepancy in the
Due to and Due from LGU accounts of the Municipality with that of its
reciprocal accounts in the books of the Province and likewise review the
records of the Barangay pertaining to these accounts and to make the
necessary adjustments to correct the balances;
b. request the Provincial Accountant to furnish her with copies of the Journal
Entry Vouchers showing the amount collected by the Province in favor of the
Municipality to serve as basis in recording the Due from LGU in the books of
the Municipality as provided under Section 20 of the Manual on NGAS for
LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
issue demand letters to the concerned accountable persons for the immediate and
full settlement of the receivables arising from unremitted collections in previous
years and to file appropriate charges against those who would refuse to settle
their obligations to the Municipality.
As to the receivables from the deceased accountable officer, we recommended
that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the filing of
request for authority from the Commission on Audit for the write-off of her
account amounting to P41,954.00, subject to compliance with all the conditions
provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure propriety in the management
of the PCF by:
a) Directing the Petty Cash Fund (PCF) Custodian to liquidate all the PCF under
his custody within 20 days after the end of the year;
b) Limiting the amount of PCF to what is only sufficient to cover the non-
recurring, emergency and petty expenses of the LGU for one month
supported with approved estimates of expenses;
c) Requiring the Petty Cash Fund (PCF) Custodian and the Municipal
Accountant to observe the proper replenishment and liquidation of the PCF
and use the forms prescribed such as Petty Cash Vouchers (PCVs) and
Report of Disbursements supported with summary/list of PCVs;
d) Directing the Municipal Accountant to properly record the transactions
pertaining to the PCF using the prescribed accounting entries, as follows:
 Release of cash advance for petty cash fund miscellaneous expenses
Petty Cash xxx
Cash in Bank – LCCA xxx
 Replenishment of petty cash fund during the year
Expenses xxx
Cash in Bank – LCCA xxx
 Liquidation at year end
Expenses xxx
Petty Cash xxx
 Return of unused Petty Cash Fund
Cash in Treasury xxx
Petty Cash xxx
e) Instructing the PCF Custodian to refrain from making payments of more than
P15,000.00 per transaction out of the PCF, otherwise such will be disallowed in
audit.

Likewise, direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit to the Office of
the Auditor the 154 unsubmitted vouchers totaling P399,167.08 to avoid
suspension of related transactions .

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to grant the Disbursing Officer cash advances equivalent
only to the net amount of payroll for the specific pay period such as every 15th
and end of the month;
b. Disbursing officer to ensure that the receipts of salaries, wages and other
personnel benefits by concerned officials and employees are properly
acknowledged through their signatures in the payrolls and to observe the
prescribed date of liquidation for advances for payroll, which is five days after
the pay period; and
c. Municipal Accountant to make sure that liquidations for advances for payrolls
are properly supported with the required documentations such as DTRs and
duly acknowledged payrolls/vouchers.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to strictly
comply with Revenue Regulation No. 2-98, Republic Act (RA) No. 8291,
Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA 7875 and 9679 by ensuring
that the amount of all contributions and taxes withheld are remitted in full on
prescribed due dates;
b. direct the Municipal Accountant to continue to make representation with the
BIR officials to reconcile both their records in order to establish the exact
withholding taxes due for remittance; and
c. direct the Municipal Accountant to exert efforts to locate old GSIS, Pag-IBIG
and PhilHealth files and/or request from the GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth
Offices for records or certification of arrears or no-arrears, to facilitate the
remittance or adjustments in the books the previous years’ outstanding
unremitted balances.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Accountant include the beginning
balances per subsidiary ledger accounts of the Due to BIR, Due to GSIS, Due to
Pag-IBIG and Due to PhilHealth to avoid incurring negative balances and to
reflect the correct amounts of the accounts at year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct concerned officials and personnel to exercise prudence in the utilization
of funds intended for meals and snacks during official meetings,
programs/projects and activities and observe the limitations or prohibitions to
reception and entertainment prescribed by Section 343 of RA 7160;
b. direct the unicipal officials and personnel to charge the costs of meals and
snacks for entertaining visitors/guests of their corresponding offices to their
monthly representation allowances, which were granted to them for that very
purpose;
c. direct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to charge the
amount of discretionary expenses to the appropriate Extraordinary and
Miscellaneous Expenses account; and
d. limit the amount spent for discretionary/extraordinary expenses to the amount
equivalent to two per cent of the actual receipts of Real Property Taxes of the
next preceding calendar year, to avoid suspension or possible disallowance in
audit.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor initiate
the formulation of policies or guidelines on the availment and entitlement of cellular
phone allowances of Municipal officials and employees, subject to the approval of the
Sanggunian Bayan Members and compliance with other requirements;; and

We strongly recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all concerned officials
and personnel adhere strictly to Section 93 of PD No. 1445 which requires
government agencies to make direct payments to suppliers and ensure that the
provisions of RA 9184 on the appropriate method of procurement and BIR
Revenue Regulation 2-98 on withholding of taxes are not circumvented.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. all officials and employees to stop the practice of renting private vehicles for
seminars, conferences, meetings, and other official business unless justified
by the prevailing circumstances as illustrated in Section 7(b) of Executive
Order No. 77 dated March 15, 2019, otherwise the related disbursements will
be suspended and/or disallowed in audit. It is recommended that instead,
officials and employees are instructed to use only the ordinary public
conveyance or customary modes of transportation and claim only the actual
transportation expenses and the Daily Travel Expenses according to the
rates prescribed under E.O. 77.
b. concerned officials to study the feasibility of instituting a policy or procedure
wherein officials and employees who need to travel on official business may
use or borrow any available municipality-owned motor vehicle on a
scheduled basis to lessen the need to hire private motor vehicles; and
c. the Municipal Accountant to make sure that all claims and/or
reimbursements of motor vehicle rentals are supported with proper and
complete documentations such as official receipts, contracts, identification
cards of drivers or service providers, travel orders, certificates of
appearance, certificates of travel completed and other documents that will
prove the necessity and propriety of the transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all concerned Municipal officers
to ensure the full implementation of projects under the twenty per cent
Development Funds by:
a. conducting extensive Preliminary Detailed Engineering (PDE) during the
planning stage of projects prior to its inclusion in the LDIP and AIP;
b. including infrastructure contracts in the formulation of Project Procurement
Management Plan (PPMP) and Annual Procurement Plan (APP) and
revisiting the procurement planning and implementation process to pinpoint
flaws for possible improvement of the system;
c. executing all projects according to plan and submit accomplishment reports
on a monthly or quarterly basis to the Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator (MPDC);
d. advising the MPDC to monitor and evaluate the implementation of the
projects and submit periodic reports to the Municipal Mayor incorporating all
issues and concerns affecting the execution of projects, copy furnished the
COA; and
e. advising the MPDC to coordinate with the implementing offices to come up
with the most effective project implementation schedule over the course of
four quarters.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Local School Board (LSB) to limit the utilisation of the SEF only to programs,
projects and activities enumerated under DEPED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1
s. 2017;
b. Municipal Budget Officer to restore the amounts inappropriately charged against
the SEF from the General Fund amounting to P286,330.01, otherwise, this will be
disallowed in audit;
c. Municipal Accountant to submit all disbursement vouchers and liquidation
reports to the Office of the Auditor amounting to P4,879,603.72, to avoid
suspension of said transactions; and
d. Municipal Accountant to ensure that all disbursements bear the approval of the
Municipal Mayor or his authorized representative and other proper authorities,
otherwise the transactions will be suspended in audit.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant draw the necessary Journal Entry to adjust the RPT and SET Receivables balances of the RPT and SET Receivables accounts. The Provincial Government of Cavite had met the Municipal Mayor to discuss the option of holding Public Auction for delinquent taxpayers. Continued collection of taxes under the MOA.
observations.reason: There was no coordination between the Treasurer’s Office and the barangays. Receivables were not yet fully collected.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and the acting GSO agreed with the recommendations and committed to implement them in the succeeding years. The Municipality created an Inventory Team. Vacant positions were filled-up. The Municipal Accountant will prepare ledger cards starting July 2020.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Together with the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer continue to make representation with the BIR officials to reconcile both their records in order to establish the exact withholding taxes due for remittance;
b. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately remit all the tax due upon determination from the BIR the exact amount due to it; and
c. Require the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to henceforth ensure that all taxes withheld be remitted to the BIR on or before the tenth (10th) day of the month following the month in which the withholding was made and to prevent incurrence of penalties and surcharges due to late remittances.

observations.recommendation:

a) Cause the immediate implementation of all priority projects, past and current years, of the Municipality were funded by the 20 per cent DF by seeing to it that the known causes/reasons for its delayed implementation like lack of manpower is promptly addressed;
b) Strictly observe timelines for future project implementations to ensure that the benefits that could be derived therefrom are availed of by the intended beneficiaries at the earliest possible time; and
c) Prioritize the addition of staff in the MPDC Office to help and assist the Municipal Planning and Development in the preparation of work programs, building plans and other related engineering tasks.

observations.reason: Other projects programmed for the year and previous years remained unimplemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting General Services Officer and/or the BAC Secretariat to furnish the Audit Team with copies of approved contracts and all the supporting documents within five days from their execution as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009, to facilitate the prompt review and evaluation of the contracts and the early communication to management on the deficiencies noted, if any.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and other concerned officers were instructed to furnish the Office of the Auditor with the required documents. Disbursement vouchers were stamped as paid.
observations.reason: Contracts remained unsubmitted to the Office of the Auditor earlier but subsequently disbursement vouchers were stamped as paid.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer and GAD Focal Person to ensure the establishment and maintenance of a GAD database to serve as basis for a gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation. observations.managementAction: The MSWDO had attended seminar where they were required to make GAD data base. The Municipal Budget Officer had identified which expenses are chargeable to GAD fund.

observations.managementAction: Disbursement vouchers were stamped as paid.

The Municipal Accountant and PCF Custodian agreed and committed to implement the recommendations. The Municipal Mayor instructed that petty cash disbursements be monitored and replenishment procedures observed.
observations.reason: PCF was not replenished/fully regularized earlier. Some petty cash disbursements still lacked required documentations which were being addressed.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor informed that an Executive Order on the matter will be issued to regulate the use of rented cars. The Municipal Mayor and Accountant agreed to disseminate the audit observations and recommendations to all officials and employees.
observations.reason: The Municipality lacked available vehicle for official business and funds to purchase vehicle. Some measures to regulate rentals were pending issuance of Executive Order.

observations.managementAction: The title of one land procured for use of the Ternate West National High School was transferred under the name of the Municipality of Ternate. The Municipal Accountant mentioned that the Municipal Mayor will issue an Office Order directing the Municipal Assessor to coordinate with the Registry of Deeds/Bureau of Lands for the titling of the subject five lots. The Accounting Office will prepare ledger cards starting July 2020. The Municipal Treasurer and Accountant were directed to make representations with the BIR and other agencies relative to the Municipal land.
observations.reason: Other lands were not yet transferred. The concerned employees were directed to make representations to the BIR.

purposes;

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned officials and employees to settle/liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period and direct the Municipal Accountant to stop the grant of additional cash advance to officials and employees unless the previous cash advance is liquidated or settled. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Budget Officer and Treasurer find ways and means to address the cash deficit the municipality had been experiencing for several years now by exercising diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of government funds and intensifying its collection efforts. observations.reason: Problems were encountered on the implementation of projects such as right of way, change in the project proposals, and delay in the approval of the SB in the realignment of certain projects.

Thereafter, stop entering/negotiating with lending institutions/private banks except for those authorized institutions stated in Section 52 of GAA; and
require the Municipal Accountant to issue clearance to the employees before acquiring a loan from Cooperatives/Associations organized and managed by the employees of the Municipality to ensure that the monthly net take home pay of the employees is not below P3,500.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Treasurer coordinate with the management of LBP-Cavite City and/or Trece Martires City branch on how to avail the automated payroll system so that advantages/benefits may be enjoyed by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Bids and Awards Committee to invite in all stages of the procurement process, in writing, in addition to the representative of the Commission on Audit, representatives from a duly recognized private group in a sector or discipline relevant to the procurement at hand, and from a non-government organization in compliance with the IRR-A of RA No. 9184.

observations.managementAction:
Expenses were limited to actual income received.

Continuing appropriations were supported by funds available.
observations.reason: The estimated income for the year 2016 was not realized/met.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Treasurer to deposit all collections intact daily or not later than the next banking day or where collections are minimal and daily deposit thereof becomes costly and impractical, the collections shall be deposited at least once a week, or as soon as the collections reach P10,000.00 to prevent possible loss or misuse of the fund.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer, from time to time, deposits her collections on the next banking day.
observations.reason:
Besides from the voluminous 
work load, the Municipal Treasurer could not delegate the deposit of her collection to her subordinates as it is sensitive and entails 
conscientious planning.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the grant of cash advance to elected officers, except for their traveling expenses and confidential fund.  

We also recommended that all cash advances, except for petty cash fund, be fully settled or liquidated at the end of the year as required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 97-002.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant exercises restraint on the grant of cash advance, especially to those whose cash advance is not yet liquidated.
observations.reason:
Officers and employees with outstanding cash advance still preparing/
organizing the necessary documents in support of the liquidation report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Treasurer that only non-recurring, emergency and petty expenses shall be paid out of the petty cash fund.  

We also recommended that expenses not considered petty be paid directly to the payee by checks in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Section 48 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Unit.
observations.managementAction:
Management limited the use of petty cash fund to emergency and petty expenses

Purchases with significant amount were directly paid to the payee.
observations.reason: The Management in emergency cases uses petty cash for payment of recurring expenses.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that projects under the 20% Development Fund be fully implemented in accordance with the time line provided in the Annual Investment Plan in order that benefits are availed of by the intended beneficiaries at the earliest possible time.  

We also recommended maximum utilization of the fund for the benefits of the Constituents.
observations.managementAction:
Some of the projects under the 20% development funds were implemented

Management is fast tracking the implementation of projects under the development fund.
observations.reason: Lack of manpower in the engineering department that will prepare the program of works, difficulty in acquiring right of way and owners of lot suddenly changed their mind in donating the lot

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to transfer the unexpended balance of the QRF (30%) and DRRMF-MOOE from the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund under the Trust Fund books as prescribed under COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
observations.managementAction: P500,000.00 was transferred to the Special Trust Fund under the Trust Fund books.
observations.reason: Estimated collections were not realized, thus, inability of the Municipality to transfer in full the prior years’ shares from the collection of RPT to Brgy. Sapang 1

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Assessor to submit to the Municipal Treasurer the real property tax assessment roll which will be the basis of the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant in the preparation of a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year and to set-up the correct RPT/SET receivable at the beginning of the year, respectively.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor issued a memorandum to the Municipal Assessor instructing her to furnish a copy of real property tax assessment roll to the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: The Municipal Assessor did not adhere to the memorandum of the Municipal Mayor to prepare the assessment roll, thus, the Municipal Treasurer failed to submit to the Municipal Accountant the duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant remit to the respective barangays, especially that for Brgy. Sapang 1 which involved a significant amount, the shares from the collections of real property tax in order that the barangays can use their shares for the delivery of basic and other services.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipality transferred/
remitted to the respective barangays their shares from the collections of real property tax.
observations.reason: There were cash overdraft in the general fund, thus, the Municipality could not transfer in full the prior years’ shares from the collection of RPT to Brgy. Sapang 1

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to remit the balance of taxes withheld amounting to P4,550,469.25 to the Bureau of Internal Revenue, and henceforth, remit all taxes withheld from suppliers/contractors within ten days following the end of the month the withholding was made in compliance with Section 9(c) of the Bureau of Internal Revenue Regulation No. 6-97.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality remitted the taxes withheld during the year 2016
observations.reason: Prior years’ taxes withheld were not remitted to the BIR due to unavailability of remittance list.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Bids and Awards Committee to consider the remedies provided under the Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) guidelines that governed the suspension and blacklisting of manufacturers, suppliers, distributors, contractors and consultants that habitually withdraw from bidding without valid reason.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality, through the BAC, issued a memorandum to all participating bidders that habitually withdrawing from bidding will be dealt accordingly.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Bids and Awards Committee to invite in all stages of the procurement process, in writing, in addition to the representative of the Commission on Audit, representatives from a duly recognized private group in a sector or discipline relevant to the procurement at hand, and from a non-government organization in compliance with the IRR-A of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality require the contractor to post a warranty security
observations.reason: There was no accredited private group in a sector or discipline relevant to the procurement at hand in the area of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Accountant to require the Contractor to post the necessary warranty security as required under Section 62 of the IRR-A of RA No. 9184 before final payment is made.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality require the contractor to post a warranty security

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer that all payments/checks due M.D. Biliran Construction be made in the name/drawn in favor of M.D. Biliran Construction to protect the interest of the government, otherwise, require M.D. Biliran Construction to request in writing that payments due M.D. Biliran Construction be made payable to Mr. Ramos.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality had secured a letter from the contractor certifying that the payments made by the Municipality were duly received.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Health Officer to maximize the utilization of the Per Family Payment Rate (PFPR) as required under the Philhealth Circular No. 010, s. 2012 for the promotion of quality health care services.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Health Officer prioritized the medicines needed in the Municipality.
observations.reason: Funds from DOH were received by the Municipality in the last quarter of 2016, thus, funds were not fully utilized.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advice the respective Municipal Departments Head to immediately implement the priority projects, plans and activities of the Municipality in order that benefits are availed of by their constituents at the earliest possible time.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipality implemented the projects funded under BUB.

Four (4) of the five (5) projects were already completed in CY 2016.  One (1) was still on-going.
